headline: Former Governor Ted Schwinden Dies, 2023
body:
The final curtain fell on October 7, 2023, for Theodore 'Ted' Schwinden, the nineteenth Governor of Montana. Born on the hardscrabble earth of a Roosevelt County farm, nestled within the vast expanse of the Fort Peck Indian Reservation on August 31, 1925, Schwinden’s life was a testament to the grinding realities and enduring spirit of the American West. He was a son of the soil, a valedictorian who traded textbooks for the grim theater of World War II, serving in both European and Pacific campaigns before returning home in 1946. He pursued higher education, earning degrees from the University of Montana, and even began doctoral studies at the University of Minnesota, but the call of the land, and his ailing father, pulled him back to the farm. This intimate connection to the land and its people would define his political career.

His ascent through the ranks of Montana politics was steady, marked by a relentless dedication to public service. From the Montana House of Representatives in 1958, where he served two terms, to his role as Commissioner of State Lands, and then as Lieutenant Governor under Thomas Lee Judge, Schwinden honed his understanding of the state’s intricate machinery. But it was in 1981, when he took the oath as Governor, that the true measure of the man would be tested. Montana, a state whose very sinews were woven from agriculture and mining, found itself in the vise-grip of economic despair. The global markets shifted, commodity prices plummeted, and the lifeblood of countless families began to drain away. Mines shuttered, farms faced foreclosure, and the specter of unemployment haunted every town.

Schwinden did not flinch. He met the crisis with a pragmatism born of his own hard-won experience. His administration launched the ambitious "Build Montana" economic plan, a desperate bid to diversify the state’s economy and staunch the bleeding. He was a governor who believed in direct engagement, famously taking his "Capital for a Day" events to every corner of the state, listening to the anxieties and hopes of ordinary Montanans. He understood that the people were not statistics, but flesh and blood, facing real hunger and real fear. He was a governor who spoke plainly, without pretense, a quality that resonated deeply with a populace weary of political platitudes. His commitment to fiscal responsibility was unwavering, even in the face of immense pressure. In a legislative session, addressing the very real concerns of revenue, Governor Schwinden declared, and I quote from the minutes of a 1987 Taxation Committee meeting, "I remain convinced that a sales tax is neither necessary nor desirable in Montana." This was his stance, a reflection of his deep understanding of the economic burdens already shouldered by the state’s working families.

He served two terms, from 1981 to 1989, guiding Montana through some of its most turbulent economic waters. Despite favorable polling, he chose not to seek a third term, honoring a promise he had made to the people. His passing at the age of 98 in Phoenix, Arizona, marked the end of an era, a final chapter for a leader who had once stood as a bulwark against the tide of economic hardship, a man who never forgot the struggles of the common Montanan.
